Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed verbal phrase (noun + locative + object + transitive verb)
Figurative Meaning: To carry emotional burden silently without expressing pain.
Usage Contexts: Family conflict, personal struggle, emotional narration, literature
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common in Godāvari belt oral laments describing inner suffering without public display.
First Example Sentence: Āme gundē mēda pāta peṭṭukoni inta kālam māṭlāḍalēdu.
Second Example Sentence: Āyana gundē mēda pāta peṭṭi andarini navvincāḍu.
Third Example Sentence: Nīvu gundē mēda pāta peṭṭakunda nāto ceppu.

Figurative Meaning: To realize truth after ignorance or denial.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Used in village assemblies when elders correct younger members.
First Example Sentence: Ippudu kallu terici cūḍu, nijam telustundi.
Second Example Sentence: Āyana kallu terici cūsina taruvāta māṭa mārindi.
Third Example Sentence: Mīru kallu terici cūḍakapōte tappu jarugutundi.

Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Agrarian metaphor referencing monsoon clouds that gather without rain.
Second Example Sentence: Nīvu mabbulu la māṭlāḍakunda spaṣṭamga ceppu.
Third Example Sentence: Sabha lō vāranta mabbulu la māṭlāḍāru.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Compound verb phrase (noun + participle + object + verb)
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects collective agricultural labor traditions in Bhadrachalam region.
First Example Sentence: Manam cheyyi kalipi pani cēste vijayam vastundi.
Second Example Sentence: Grāmam anta cheyyi kalipi illu kattindi.
Third Example Sentence: Ā project kosam andaru cheyyi kalipi pani cēsāru.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Locative metaphorical clause (noun + locative + object + verb)
Figurative Meaning: To suppress what one truly wants to say.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Linked to folk belief associating stomach with secrecy and emotion.
First Example Sentence: Āme kaḍupu lō māṭa peṭṭukoni navvindhi.
Second Example Sentence: Nīvu kaḍupu lō māṭa peṭṭakunda māṭlāḍu.
Third Example Sentence: Āyana kaḍupu lō māṭa peṭṭi sabha ni viḍicāḍu.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominal metaphor phrase (noun + comparative particle + noun)
Figurative Meaning: Words that are firm and unchangeable.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Stone symbolism from temple-building culture along Godāvari.
Second Example Sentence: Nā māṭa rēyi la māṭa ani gurṭu peṭṭuko.
Third Example Sentence: Vāri rēyi la māṭa grāmam lō prasiddham.

Figurative Meaning: A smile that is gentle and beautiful.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Floral imagery common in tribal wedding songs.
First Example Sentence: Āme navvu puvvu la undi ani andarū ceppāru.
Second Example Sentence: Ā cinna pillā navvu puvvu la undi.
Third Example Sentence: Āyana āme navvu puvvu la undi ani prasaṁsa cēsāḍu.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Descriptive clause (locative noun + plural noun + verb)
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Monsoon-based omen metaphor in forest communities.
First Example Sentence: I rājyam mēda mabbulu kūrāyi ani vāru ceppāru.
Third Example Sentence: Grāmam mēda mabbulu kūrāyi ani peddaḷu heccarin̄cāru.

Figurative Meaning: To speak unseriously about serious matters.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from communal game culture in tribal gatherings.
First Example Sentence: I viṣayam lō āṭa āḍutunnattu māṭlāḍaku.
Second Example Sentence: Āyana āṭa āḍutunnattu māṭlāḍi samasya pen̄cāḍu.
Third Example Sentence: Mīru āṭa āḍutunnattu māṭlāḍite andaru kopam paḍutāru.

Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Wind imagery common in forest oral storytelling traditions.
First Example Sentence: Nā māṭa gāli la māṭa pōyindi.
Second Example Sentence: Ā salaha gāli la māṭa pōyindi.
Third Example Sentence: Vāri heccarika gāli la māṭa pōyindi.
